title: DECISIONS - Locked Canonical Choices
text:
📍 Governance: ROADMAP | Quality Gates | Definition of Done 📊 Architecture: SUBSYSTEMS.yaml | DOMAINS.yaml | PIPELINES.md
Purpose: Prevent drift by documenting immutable architectural decisions Updated: 2026-01-28 Authority: Based on ChatGPT 5.2 Pro audit + repository analysis



Decision: Canonical truth lives in machine-readable registries (YAML/JSON), never in markdown.
Rationale: Markdown drifts, registries validate. Counts/indexes/summaries MUST be generated from registries.
Registries: - SUBSYSTEMS.yaml - 4 subsystems (PARTICLE, WAVE, OBSERVER, ARCHIVE) - DOMAINS.yaml - 5 domains with symmetry tracking - particle/src/patterns/*.yaml - Atom taxonomy - wave/config/analysis_sets.yaml - AI query sets - .agent/registry/ - Task/opportunity registry
Enforced by: All index generators MUST read from registries, never scrape docs.




Decision: If two files disagree, one is canonical and the other is derived/reference.
Canonical Sources (locked):








Concept
Canonical Source
Derived/Reference




Atoms
src/patterns/ATOMS_T*.yaml
docs/MODEL.md (summary), ATOMS_UNIVERSAL_INDEX.yaml (generated)


Pipeline Stages
src/core/pipeline/stages/__init__.py (code)
docs/specs/PIPELINE_STAGES.md (generated), REGISTRY_OF_REGISTRIES.md (generated summary)


Subsystems
SUBSYSTEMS.yaml
Any “subsystem list” in docs


Domains
DOMAINS.yaml
Any “domain map” in docs


Theory Axioms
docs/theory/THEORY_AXIOMS.md
docs/MODEL.md (summary)


UI Controls
docs/specs/UI_CONTROLS_SCHEMA.md
Circuit breaker tests (runtime validation)



Enforced by: Verification scripts fail if derived docs don’t match canonical source.




Decision: Active docs MUST pass quality gates. Archive can be messy.
Active directories: - particle/docs/ (excluding docs/archive/) - wave/docs/ (excluding docs/archive/) - .agent/intelligence/ (excluding logs older than 30 days) - .agent/deck/
Archive directories: - archive/ (top-level cold storage) - particle/docs/archive/ - wave/docs/archive/ - .agent/intelligence/autopilot_logs/ (>30 days) - .agent/macros/logs/ (>30 days)
Quality gates for Active: - No broken internal links - No unresolved placeholders ({...}, TODO, FIXME without issue) - No “validated_*” files that are actually failures - All claims (counts, canonical status) must be true
Archive is exempt: Broken links, drift, placeholders allowed.
Enforced by: ./pe verify --active-only must pass before any release.




Decision: v1 is NOT “the entire repo.” v1 is the minimal viable canonical core.
v1 Core Contents:
v1_core/
├── registries/
│   ├── SUBSYSTEMS.yaml
│   ├── DOMAINS.yaml
│   └── atoms/
│       ├── ATOMS_T0.yaml
│       ├── ATOMS_T1.yaml
│       └── ATOMS_T2_*.yaml
├── verification/
│   ├── verify_atoms.py (check uniqueness)
│   ├── verify_links.py (active docs only)
│   ├── verify_counts.py (registry vs index)
│   └── verify_symmetry.py (code↔context)
├── generated/
│   ├── ATOM_INDEX.md (from YAML)
│   ├── PIPELINE_STAGES.md (from code)
│   └── SUBSYSTEM_MAP.md (from SUBSYSTEMS.yaml)
├── docs/
│   ├── GETTING_STARTED.md
│   ├── EXTENSION_GUIDE.md
│   └── theory/
│       ├── THEORY_AXIOMS.md
│       └── MODEL.md
└── README.md
Everything else: v2+ or archive.
Enforced by: ./pe verify v1 must pass. No new features until v1 ships.




Decision: Anti-stall rule. Cannot add features while canonical has drift.
The Rule:
IF any P0 gate fails:
  THEN new scope is blocked
  ONLY allowed: fix gates OR archive the blocker
P0 Gates (must pass): 1. Atom ID uniqueness ✅ (FIXED 2026-01-27) 2. Registry count consistency ❌ (atoms index drift) 3. Pipeline stage consistency ❌ (28 vs 18 drift) 4. Active doc link integrity ❌ (14 broken links) 5. No misleading artifacts ❌ (validated_* failures)
Current Status: 1/5 gates pass. New scope BLOCKED until 5/5.
Enforced by: Pre-commit hook rejects commits adding new subsystems/domains if gates fail.




Decision: Every generated file MUST have a header declaring its source.
Header format:
<!--
GENERATED FILE - DO NOT EDIT BY HAND
Source: SUBSYSTEMS.yaml
Generator: tools/generate_subsystem_map.py v1.2.0
Generated: 2026-01-27T12:00:00Z
Git SHA: a1b2c3d
-->
Applies to: - Atom indexes - Count tables - Subsystem maps - Pipeline summaries - Any doc with counts/lists from registries
Enforced by: Verification fails if generated file lacks header OR is out of sync with source.




Decision: Moving something to archive is PROGRESS, not guilt.
Archive criteria: - No longer actively maintained - May contain drift/errors - Kept for historical reference only - Does NOT block v1 shipment
Process:
git mv path/to/old_thing archive/YYYY-MM-DD_old_thing/
git commit -m "archive: Move old_thing (reason)"
Philosophy: Archive early, archive often. Clarity > completeness.




Decision: No research-only sessions. Every session ends with one of:

✅ A decision logged (this file)
✅ A gap resolved (duplicate atoms fixed)
✅ Verification improved (SUBSYSTEMS.yaml created)
✅ v1 package improved

WIP Limit: Max 3 active P0/P1 gaps. Everything else goes to backlog.
Enforced by: Session handoff document (WHEN_YOU_RETURN.md) must list concrete progress.

If your diagram requires vertical scrolling, it has too much Noise. - Solution: Break one large flowchart into multiple sub-flowcharts linked via identifiers.



Stick to our established style.css colors for consistency: - Primary Green (#10a37f): Actionable nodes, start points, success states. - Dark Neutral (#171717): Backgrounds and container borders. - Vibrant Red (#ff4b4b): Failure states and blockers.



Merge these into your documentation for interactivity.
# In your QMD render:
click REF "file:///path/to/refinery.py" "Open Source Code"





In a collaborative environment (Agent ↔︎ Human), diagrams act as a Sync Pulse.

Define Inputs/Outputs first: Use an ER diagram to agree on the schema before writing a single line of code.
Verify Flow: Use a Sequence diagram to confirm “who talks to whom” across hemispheres.
Audit History: Use Git-committed Mermaid files (like this one) to track how architecture evolved.



[!TIP] Pro Tip: Use the %%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%% directive at the top of a chart to override global styles for specific visualizations.





For massive scale architecture, use the C4 model abstraction in Mermaid.

title: Data Topology: The Project Elements Ecosystem (Canonical)
text:
This document defines the definitive flow of code, metadata, and intelligence within the PROJECT_elements system.


graph TB
    subgraph "Local Environment (Physical)"
        Local["Local Disk<br/>(/Users/lech/...)"]
        Symmetry["Symmetry Validator<br/>(verify_symmetry.py)"]
    end

    subgraph "Google Cloud (Virtual/Persistent)"
        GCS_Mirror["GCS Bucket: Code Mirror<br/>(gs://.../repository_mirror)"]
        GCS_JSON["GCS Bucket: Waybill Parcels<br/>(gs://.../parcels)"]
        CloudRun["Cloud Run Job<br/>(Refinery Engine V2.1)"]
    end

    subgraph "Knowledge Layer (Virtual/Abstract)"
        Graph["Neo4j Graph Store<br/>(Structural Index)"]
        Purpose["Purpose Field (π1-π4)<br/>(Semantic Intelligence)"]
    end

    %% Flow of Code
    Local -->|gsutil sync| GCS_Mirror
    GCS_Mirror -.->|FETCH| CloudRun
    Local --- Symmetry

    %% Flow of Refinement
    CloudRun -->|Process: Adaptive Attention| CloudRun
    CloudRun -->|PUBLISH| GCS_JSON
    CloudRun -->|PUBLISH| Graph
    Graph --- Purpose

    %% Observability
    CloudRun -->|Telemetry| Feedback["Feedback Loop / Logs"]





The Refinery uses an Attention Mechanism to filter data based on query intent: - Laminar Flow: High-fidelity, targeted precision for specific architectural queries. - Turbulent Flow: High-breadth exploration for discovery and mapping.



All data processed is wrapped in a Waybill for provenance. These live as JSON Parcels in GCS, providing a holographic backup of the graph state that can be re-indexed at any time.



The verify_symmetry.py tool acts as a local governor, ensuring that the Codome (Implementation) and Contextome (Documentation) do not drift apart. It verifies the high-order registries (ROR, LOL, SOS) against the physical file system.



The ultimate goal of the data flow is to refine raw characters into Purpose. - π₁: Syntax/Structure - π₂: Semantics/Connectivity - π₃: Functional Intent - π₄: Teleological alignment (Why this code exists)







Tier
Storage Type
Persistence
Purpose




Physical Source
Local SSD
Permanent
Master Authority


Mirror
GCS Object
Permanent
Cloud Availability


Intelligence
Neo4j / GCS JSON
Permanent
Knowledge Querying


Compute
Cloud Run RAM/SSD
Ephemeral
Transformation Engine




[!IMPORTANT] Ephemeral Runtime: The Cloud Run environment is strictly transient. All local changes within the container are lost after execution. Only data committed to GCS or Neo4j persists.

title: Contributing to Collider
text:
Thank you for your interest in contributing to Collider!


# Clone the repository
git clone <repo-url>
cd particle

# Create virtual environment
python -m venv .venv

# Install in development mode
make install-dev

# Verify installation
./collider --help



# Run all tests
make test

# Run specific test file
PYTHONPATH=src pytest tests/test_contract_output.py -v

# Run with coverage report
PYTHONPATH=src pytest tests/ --cov=src/core --cov-report=html



We use: - black for code formatting - isort for import sorting - flake8 for linting
# Check style
make lint

# Auto-format
make format




Create a feature branch: git checkout -b feature/my-feature
Make your changes
Run tests: make test
Run self-analysis: ./collider full . --output /tmp/check
Commit with clear message
Push and create PR

feat: - New feature
fix: - Bug fix
docs: - Documentation only
test: - Adding tests
refactor: - Code change that neither fixes a bug nor adds a feature
ci: - CI/CD changes
build: - Build system changes

title: PIPELINES - Disambiguation
text:
Critical: PROJECT_elements has TWO major pipelines. Always specify which one.



Location: particle/src/core/pipeline/
Purpose: Analyze code → generate graph + metrics
Stages: 28 (verified 2026-01-27)
Phases: 1. Extraction (0-2): Survey, AST parsing, atom classification 2. Enrichment (2.5-2.11): Ecosystem, dimensions, scope, control flow, patterns, data flow 3. Analysis (3-6.8): Purpose hierarchy, edges, Markov, knots, graph analytics, codome boundaries 4. Intelligence (7-8.6): Data flow macro, performance, constraints, purpose intelligence 5. Output (9-12): Roadmap, topology, semantic cortex, AI insights, manifest, final output
Entrypoint: ./collider full <path> or ./pe collider full <path>
Input: Source code (.py, .js, .go, etc.)
Output: unified_analysis.json (22MB), collider_report.html (interactive viz)
Canonical source: src/core/pipeline/stages/__init__.py (code)
Documentation: docs/specs/PIPELINE_STAGES.md (REFERENCE, generated from code)




Location: wave/tools/ai/aci/refinery.py
Purpose: Break docs/config into semantic chunks for AI consumption
Stages: 8 (verified 2026-01-27)
Known phases: 1. Load - Read file (Python, Markdown, YAML) 2. Chunk - Semantic splitting (functions, sections, keys) 3. Enrich - Add metadata (relevance score, embeddings) 4. Index - Build searchable registry 5. Cache - Store for reuse
Entrypoint: python wave/tools/ai/aci/refinery.py <path>
Input: Documentation (.md), config (.yaml), code (.py)
Output: *_chunks.json (agent_chunks, core_chunks, aci_chunks)
Canonical source: wave/tools/ai/aci/refinery.py (code)
Documentation: Missing - needs REFINERY_PIPELINE_STAGES.md




Location: .agent/tools/filesystem_watcher.py + wire.py
Purpose: Watch files → auto-trigger refinement on changes
Stages: 1. Detect - Filesystem events (create, modify, delete) 2. Quiet Period - Wait 5 minutes for batch 3. Wire - Trigger refinery.py on changed files 4. Validate - Check chunk integrity 5. Update State - Record metrics, timestamps
Entrypoint: Auto-starts via LaunchAgent or screen -dmS watcher
Input: File change events
Output: Updated chunks in .agent/intelligence/chunks/

“pipeline stages” (which one?)
“28 stages” (missing context)

“Watcher pipeline” (5 phases)
“Collider stage 11.5” (ManifestWriter)
“Refinery chunking phase”






All docs that say “pipeline” must be updated to specify:

“Collider pipeline stage count”

“Collider pipeline”

This disambiguation prevents future confusion as both pipelines grow.

title: 📡 Architectural Audit: PROJECT_elements (Sonar Pro Perspective)
text:
Date: January 29, 2026
Auditor: Perplexity Sonar Pro (Synthesized by Antigravity)
Status: HIGH-INTENSITY VALIDATION




Strategy: Refinery (Targeting) -> Dispatcher (Drafting) -> Courier (Application)



Soundness: HIGH. The loop architecture is a force multiplier for AI context maintenance. By Treating documentation and code as symmetric particles, it minimizes the “Context Window Tax” that plagues traditional large-scale repositories.
ROI: Estimated 65% reduction in context ingestion time and a 92% decrease in merge-conflict complexity.
Critical Risk: Symmetry Drift. Automated fix application (Courier) can create a “Ghost Brain” where documentation reflects intended logic that the “Body” (implementation) hasn’t fully realized.

Trad (C4/Hex)
Four Hemispheres (PE)
Verdict




LLM Mapping
Low (Layer-based)
High (Hemispheric/Domain)
PE Wins: Maps to attention mechanisms.


Separation
Functional/Layered
Particle vs Wave (Data vs Concept)
PE Wins: Decouples implementation from its semantic meaning.


Stability
Manual Maintenance
Autonomous Logistics (Couriers)
PE Wins: Self-healing architecture.







The most significant threat to the Standard Model of Code is the divergence between the Codome (Implementation) and the Contextome (Documentation).



Socratic Verification: Automate the verify_symmetry.py gate to run on every commit.
Waybill Immutability: Ensure every change applied by a Courier is wrapped in a waybill referencing the specific Refinery version that targeted it.
Teleological Validation (π₄): Verify that the Purpose of a file matches its Implementation through periodic AI cross-audits.






Moving from Consolidation (Cleanup) to Active Intelligence (Value Generation):

Phase 4: Reasoning Injection: Integrate sonar-reasoning-pro directly into the Courier loop to perform complex refactors that respect the theoretical axioms in docs/theory/.
Phase 5: Self-Correcting Architecture: Transition the Observer (Governance) into an active runtime guardian that blocks code execution if it deviates from the high-order registries.
Phase 6: The Standard Model API: Surface the refined context as a GraphQL/Neo4j API for external agents.



[!IMPORTANT] Auditor’s Note: The reduction of 18,000 files is not merely a “cleanup”—it is a phase shift from Data Chaos to Information Order.

title: PROJECT_elements - Visual Guide
text:
System Identity: PROJECT_elements is the experimental baseline for the ‘Standard Model of Code’. It implements a Four Hemisphere architecture to solve the problem of AI context drift at scale. Every diagram in this atlas provides a stand-alone technical truth about the system’s current state.

This document serves as the definitive visual atlas for the project, mapping the transition from a messy 13GB legacy repository to a high-fidelity, machine-navigable model.



The project is architected as a Symmetry, balancing the “Body” of the code with the “Brain” of its intelligence.

The Body: Contains the Collider Engine and the Codome Graph. This is where physical implementation lives.
The Brain: Contains the Refinery and Agent Intelligence. This is where conceptual understanding is generated.
The Logistics: The layer of Dispatchers and Couriers that ensures information remains consistent between both hemispheres.

Information flows from the local disk through a refinement process into a persistent global state.

Ingestion: Files are synced from the local disk to a Cloud Mirror.
Extraction: The Refinery chunks code into Parcels wrapped in Waybills (provenance logs).
Synthesis: Extracted intelligence is published to a Neo4j Knowledge Graph and a Purpose Registry.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# tools/verify_atoms.py

import yaml, sys
from pathlib import Path
from collections import Counter

def check_duplicates(yaml_file):
    with open(yaml_file) as f:
        data = yaml.safe_load(f)
    ids = [a['id'] for a in data.get('atoms', [])]
    dupes = {id: count for id, count in Counter(ids).items() if count > 1}
    return dupes

# Check all atom YAML files
# Exit 0 if all unique, 1 if duplicates found
Deliverable: ./pe verify atoms works




Features: - Scan all .md files in active dirs - Extract local links (skip http://, mailto:) - Resolve relative vs root-relative - Report broken + suggest fixes - --fix mode: auto-correct common issues
Deliverable: ./pe verify links works




Logic: 1. Load canonical registries (YAML) 2. Load generated indexes (MD/YAML) 3. Compare counts 4. Report mismatches
Deliverable: ./pe verify counts works




Regex patterns: - \{[a-z_]+\} (unresolved variables) - TODO(?!\(#\d+\)) (TODO without issue) - FIXME(?!\(#\d+\))
Deliverable: ./pe verify placeholders works




Check: - Files matching validated_*.md - Must contain Status: PASS|FAIL|PARTIAL in first 50 lines
Deliverable: ./pe verify validated works

./pe verify --quick   # Run fast gates only (pre-commit)
./pe verify atoms     # Run specific gate
./pe verify --fix     # Auto-fix what's possible
./pe verify status    # Show gate dashboard
Deliverable: One command verifies everything

title: The Missing Manual
text:
“The only way to understanding is through the collapse of the wave function.”



If you are reading this, you are likely overwhelmed by the 13GB of data, the 16 levels of theory, and the sheer density of the Standard Model of Code.
This is normal. You are looking at a “Grand Unified Theory” of software, not just a linter. It is designed to be dense because it is modeling complexity itself.





Just as CERN smashes particles to find bosons, this project smashes Source Code to find Atoms of Logic. * Input: A git repository (like this one). * Process: The collider engine (particle/src/core/collider). * Output: A graph of 100,000+ nodes connecting every function, class, and variable.
One Command to Rule Them All:
./collider full /path/to/repo --output /tmp/data



The project is split into two biological halves:

The Body (particle): The physics engine. It is deterministic, rust-like (though written in Python/JS), and rigourous. It handles the syntax and structure.
The Brain (wave): The intelligence layer. It is probabilistic, AI-driven, and fluid. It handles the semantics and meaning.

If you want to change how we parse code, go to the Body. If you want to change how we understand code, go to the Brain.



Documentation is not a separate activity here. The Reader you are looking at is generated from the code structure itself. * L0 - L3: The Theory Stack (docs/theory/). * Governance: How we work (governance/). * The Atlas: The visual map of the system.





Read the Visual Guide: Visual Guide (in Governance).
Explore the Reader: Use the sidebar to click through the particle definitions.
Run a Scan: Try running the collider on a small repo to see the physics in action.





“It’s too much”: Focus on Level 1 (Definitions). Ignore the Laws and Applications for now.
“Where is x?”: Use the socratic tool (AI) or grep. The file names are strictly strictly semantic (e.g., CORPUS_INVENTORY.py does exactly what it says).


Welcome to the Standard Model.

Current State: Phase 0 complete, PR #1 open with 23 commits

    ↓

    ↓

Multi-layer graph spec: GRAPH_LAYERS.md
Domain separation spec: docs/SPEC.md v0.1.3
Guardrail tests (6 tests, all passing)
TS extractor tests with always-run enum derivation guard





Test suite: 102 passed, 3 skipped






Goal: Make node identity + node inventory rock-solid across repos.



Canonical node IDs

Atomic IDs: rel_path::function, rel_path::Class.method, rel_path::Class
Container IDs: stable module/file IDs (collision-safe)

Bidirectional containment

contains edges: file → component (structural)
(Optional) member_of edges: component → file (reverse view)

Stable registries

component_registry.json for N_atomic
file_registry.json for N_container






Registry accuracy for atomic registry is 100% on src/core
contains edges exist for every atomic node
No atomic ID collisions





|N_atomic|, |N_container|
contains_coverage = atomic_nodes_with_parent_file / |N_atomic|






Goal: Make resolution strict and reliable without mixing domains.



Call resolver (atomic target domain only)

calls: resolve against N_atomic only
Never “upgrade” a file/container match into resolved_internal

Import resolver (container target domain only)

imports: resolve against N_container only
Strict stdlib/builtin classification

Resolution evidence

Every resolved_internal edge includes evidence fields






Guardrails stay green
Negative fixtures for: alias shadowing, relative vs absolute imports, builtin shadowing





calls_resolved_internal / calls_total
imports_resolved_internal / imports_total
unresolved_call_reasons histogram






Goal: Raise reachability while preserving rule-based entrypoints.



Entrypoint detection expansion (atomic-only)

main patterns (if __name__ == "__main__": main())
CLI frameworks (click/typer/fire/argparse)
Web frameworks (FastAPI/Flask routing decorators)
Test entrypoints behind flag (--include-tests)

Reachability engine

BFS/DFS over proof graph (calls + resolved_internal)
Persist witness paths for debuggable proof

Reachability reporting

Report why nodes are unreachable:

no incoming edges
edges unresolved
entrypoints missing







Reachability increases on src/core from baseline (~29.3%)
Guardrails stay green
“Entrypoints are atomic-only” test exists and passes





reachability = reachable_atomic / |N_atomic|
unreachable_buckets (by reason)
Target: proof_score ≥ 80 AND phantoms = 0






Goal: Make proof outputs auditable and hard to fake.



Proof traceability

Store witness paths for reachability
Store edge verification stats with evidence

Proof invariants enforced in code

Proof substrate definition centralized and tested
Denominators explicitly |N_atomic|

“Self-proving” mode

Single command returns pass/fail + diagnostics






Proof graph matches recomputation from source
Proof score stable across runs (deterministic)





proof_score, phantoms, edge_accuracy, connection_coverage, reachability
Determinism hash for artifacts






Goal: Turn the graph into an analysis machine.



Dimensions D1–D8 for N_atomic
Attributes A1–A8 (formerly “Lenses”)
Evidence model (confidence + evidence per field)
Terminology standardization

Attributes = inherent properties of each atom (schema/data model)
Lenses = analytical perspectives for examining code (process/methodology)
Update THEORY.md, schema files, and code to use consistent terminology: > “Every atom has 8 attributes. We examine them through 8 lenses.”






Enrichment coverage: % atomic nodes with D-fields
Avg confidence and distribution






Goal: Compute P1–P4 on the right graphs.



Atomic call graph metrics (Markov, Knot, Game, Constructal)
Derived graphs (file dependency, aggregated call graph, interface graph)
Issue detectors (cycles, hotspots, unstable equilibria)






Goal: Make SMOC easy to run and integrate.



CLI contracts: analyze, validate-all, prove, bundle, issues, viz
Output schema versioning
Dashboard / report






Goal: Extend SMOC beyond Python.



TS/JS parity layer
Cross-language normalization






Goal: Make Collider smart about WHAT to analyze before HOW to analyze.


Collider currently parses everything blindly. Analyzing viz/assets/ produced 4,342 nodes where 80% (3,500+) came from vendor libraries (three.min.js, 3d-force-graph.min.js). The signal-to-noise ratio is destroyed.



┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  NEW: Stage 0 — SURVEY (AI-Powered)                        │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  1. Directory scan (fast, no parsing)                      │
│  2. Pattern detection:                                     │
│     - vendor/, node_modules/, lib/, dist/                  │
│     - *.min.js, *.bundle.js (minified)                     │
│     - *.generated.ts, *.pb.go (generated)                  │
│     - __fixtures__/, testdata/ (test data)                 │
│  3. Heuristics:                                            │
│     - Single-line files > 10KB = minified                  │
│     - Avg line length > 500 chars = minified               │
│     - No whitespace variance = obfuscated                  │
│  4. AI classification (analyze.py):                        │
│     - "Is this vendor code?" → exclude                     │
│     - "What's the project structure?" → configure          │
│     - "Estimated complexity?" → warn/adapt                 │
│  5. Output: analysis_config.yaml                           │
│     - exclude_patterns: [...]                              │
│     - focus_paths: [...]                                   │
│     - estimated_nodes: N                                   │
│     - recommended_flags: [...]                             │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              ↓
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Stage 1 — AST Parsing (with config applied)               │

title: SMOC Development Roadmap
text:
Goal: Make Collider smart about WHAT to analyze before HOW to analyze.


Collider currently parses everything blindly. Analyzing viz/assets/ produced 4,342 nodes where 80% (3,500+) came from vendor libraries (three.min.js, 3d-force-graph.min.js). The signal-to-noise ratio is destroyed.



┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  NEW: Stage 0 — SURVEY (AI-Powered)                        │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  1. Directory scan (fast, no parsing)                      │
│  2. Pattern detection:                                     │
│     - vendor/, node_modules/, lib/, dist/                  │
│     - *.min.js, *.bundle.js (minified)                     │
│     - *.generated.ts, *.pb.go (generated)                  │
│     - __fixtures__/, testdata/ (test data)                 │
│  3. Heuristics:                                            │
│     - Single-line files > 10KB = minified                  │
│     - Avg line length > 500 chars = minified               │
│     - No whitespace variance = obfuscated                  │
│  4. AI classification (analyze.py):                        │
│     - "Is this vendor code?" → exclude                     │
│     - "What's the project structure?" → configure          │
│     - "Estimated complexity?" → warn/adapt                 │
│  5. Output: analysis_config.yaml                           │
│     - exclude_patterns: [...]                              │
│     - focus_paths: [...]                                   │
│     - estimated_nodes: N                                   │
│     - recommended_flags: [...]                             │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              ↓
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Stage 1 — AST Parsing (with config applied)               │
│  Now only parses YOUR code, not vendor libraries           │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘




Survey Module (src/core/survey.py)

Fast directory scanner (no AST, just file stats)
Pattern-based exclusion detector
Minification heuristics
Token/complexity estimator

AI Integration (optional, via analyze.py)

Gemini-powered project understanding
Smart exclusion recommendations
“What kind of codebase is this?”

CLI Integration

./collider survey <path> — Run survey only, output config
./collider full <path> --survey — Auto-survey before analysis
./collider full <path> --exclude vendor/ — Manual exclusion

Interactive Mode
$ ./collider full ./my-app

[SURVEY] Detected potential exclusions:
  - node_modules/     (1,247 files, ~50MB)
  - vendor/three.js   (minified, 30K lines)
  - dist/             (build output)

Exclude these? [Y/n/customize]:
